title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
The goal of this project is to identify which lifestyle, sleep, and work-related variables are most strongly associated with well-being, stress, and productivity. The analysis is based on five separate datasets, each representing a different population and context (Kumar 2025; Tharmalingam 2023; Mnaadi 2023; Rogg 2023; A. 2024). Because the datasets come from different sources and cannot be meaningfully merged, each one is analysed independently; the results are then compared in Section 5 to surface recurring themes.
The two analytical methods applied throughout are Pearson correlation (described in Section 3.1) and Principal Component Analysis (described in Section 3.3). Both methods are implemented using the scikit-learn library (Pedregosa et al. 2011) and data manipulation relies on pandas (McKinney 2010).

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
3.1 Correlation Analysis
For each dataset, the Pearson correlation coefficient is computed between every numeric predictor and the selected target variable. The Pearson coefficient measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two continuous variables and is defined as:
\[
r_{xy} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}
              {\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \cdot
               \sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
\tag{1}\]
where \(x_i\) and \(y_i\) are paired observations, and \(\bar{x}\), \(\bar{y}\) are their respective sample means. A value of \(r = +1\) indicates a perfect positive linear relationship; \(r = -1\) a perfect negative one; \(r = 0\) no linear association. Results should not be interpreted as evidence of causation.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
3.2 Standardisation
Before PCA, all predictor columns are standardised to zero mean and unit variance using the z-score transformation defined in Equation 2. This step is essential because PCA is sensitive to the scale of the input variables — without standardisation, variables with large numeric ranges would dominate the principal components regardless of their statistical relevance.
\[
z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}
\tag{2}\]
where \(\mu\) is the sample mean and \(\sigma\) is the sample standard deviation of the variable.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
3.3 Principal Component Analysis
PCA is an unsupervised dimensionality-reduction method that transforms the original \(p\) numeric variables into a new orthogonal coordinate system (Jolliffe and Cadima 2016). The first principal component (PC1) captures the largest possible fraction of the total variance; PC2 captures the most remaining variance while being orthogonal to PC1, and so on.
The fraction of total variance explained by component \(k\) is called the explained variance ratio (EVR):
\[
\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}
\tag{3}\]
where \(\lambda_k\) is the \(k\)-th eigenvalue of the covariance matrix of the standardised data (see Equation 2 for standardisation).
Three PCA outputs are reported for each dataset: (1) the explained variance plot (Equation 3), (2) the PC1 loadings, and (3) a scatter of observations in the PC1–PC2 plane.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
4.1 Dataset 1: Lifestyle and Wellness
Target variable: wellness | Source: Kumar (2025)


Code
corr_table(df_pca, "wellness")




Table 2: Pearson correlations of lifestyle variables with wellness. Blue bars (positive r) indicate variables associated with higher wellness; red bars (negative r) indicate variables associated with lower wellness.






Feature
Pearson r




productivity
0.902


sleep_quality
0.75


sleep_hours
0.581


exercise_min
0.229


social_hours
0.07


screen_time_hours
-0.636


stress_level
-0.914








As shown in Table 2, sleep quality and productivity are the variables most positively associated with the wellness score, while stress level shows the strongest negative correlation, suggesting that higher stress is consistently linked to lower well-being in this dataset.


Code
plot_correlations(df_pca, "wellness", "Lifestyle: Correlation with Wellness")








Figure 1: Pearson correlations between lifestyle variables and wellness. Bars to the right indicate positive associations; bars to the left indicate negative associations.





Figure 1 visualises the same information graphically. The magnitude of the bar reflects the strength of the association; the sign indicates its direction. Variables with absolute \(r\) values above 0.3 are considered practically meaningful in exploratory analyses.


Code
scores_pca, loadings_pca, vr_pca = run_pca(df_pca, "wellness")
plot_variance(vr_pca, "Lifestyle: PCA Explained Variance")








Figure 2: PCA explained variance for the lifestyle dataset. Bars show per-component variance; the line shows cumulative variance. The first two components together explain a substantial fraction of total variability.





Figure 2 shows how the total variance is distributed across principal components. If the first two or three components account for a large share of the total variance, the dataset has a compact low-dimensional structure that can be summarised without losing much information.


Code
plot_loadings(loadings_pca, "Lifestyle: PC1 Loadings")








Figure 3: PC1 loadings for the lifestyle dataset. Large absolute loading values indicate variables that contribute most strongly to the dominant pattern.





Figure 3 reveals which variables drive PC1 (the direction of maximum variance). Variables with loadings of the same sign move together, while variables with opposite signs move in opposing directions within the dominant pattern.


Code
plot_pc_scatter(scores_pca, "Lifestyle: PC1 vs PC2")








Figure 4: PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the lifestyle dataset. Each point represents one observation projected into the two-dimensional PCA space.





Figure 4 projects all observations into the PC1–PC2 plane. Visible clusters or gradients would suggest meaningful subgroups in the data; a uniform cloud would indicate that variation is continuous and unsegmented.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
4.2 Dataset 2: Productivity and Daily Habits
Target variable: productivity | Source: Mnaadi (2023)


Code
corr_table(df_prod, "productivity")




Table 3: Pearson correlations of daily-habit variables with productivity (n = 20 000).






Feature
Pearson r




mood
0.555


sleep_hours
0.498


diet_quality
0.484


daily_exercise_mins
0.206


screen_time_hours
-0.18


stress_level
-0.56








Table 3 summarises the linear associations for the productivity dataset. With 20 000 observations, even small correlation coefficients can be statistically significant, so magnitude should be assessed alongside direction.


Code
plot_correlations(df_prod, "productivity", "Productivity: Correlation with Productivity")








Figure 5: Pearson correlations between daily-habit variables and productivity.





The pattern in Figure 5 indicates which daily habits have the most direct linear relationship with productivity. Variables that appear repeatedly across datasets (e.g., sleep, stress) provide stronger evidence for their role in well-being.


Code
scores_prod, loadings_prod, vr_prod = run_pca(df_prod, "productivity")
plot_variance(vr_prod, "Productivity: PCA Explained Variance")








Figure 6: PCA explained variance for the productivity dataset.





Figure 6 shows the variance distribution across principal components for the productivity dataset. The shape of the cumulative curve reveals how quickly the most important dimensions can account for the majority of variability.


Code
plot_loadings(loadings_prod, "Productivity: PC1 Loadings")








Figure 7: PC1 loadings for the productivity dataset.





Figure 7 identifies which predictors contribute most to PC1. If multiple lifestyle-related variables (sleep, stress, exercise) load strongly on the same component, this supports the hypothesis that well-being is multi-factorial rather than driven by a single cause.


Code
plot_pc_scatter(scores_prod, "Productivity: PC1 vs PC2")








Figure 8: PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the productivity dataset (random subsample of 3 000 observations).





Due to the large sample size, Figure 8 displays a random subsample of 3 000 observations to keep the plot readable without losing the overall distributional shape.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
4.3 Dataset 3: Sleep and Stress
Target variable: stress_level | Source: Tharmalingam (2023)


Code
corr_table(df_sleep, "stress_level")




Table 4: Pearson correlations of sleep and health variables with stress_level. Non-numeric columns (gender, occupation, BMI category, blood pressure, sleep disorder) are excluded from the analysis.






Feature
Pearson r




heart_rate
0.67


daily_steps
0.187


physical_activity_level
-0.034


age
-0.422


sleep_duration
-0.811


quality_of_sleep
-0.899








As Table 4 shows, sleep quality and duration tend to correlate negatively with stress level (higher sleep quality → lower stress), while variables like heart rate may exhibit a positive association. Note that only numeric columns are included; categorical variables such as occupation and BMI category are excluded.


Code
plot_correlations(df_sleep, "stress_level", "Sleep: Correlation with Stress Level")








Figure 9: Pearson correlations between numeric sleep/health variables and stress_level.





Figure 9 provides a visual overview of correlation directions. This dataset is particularly relevant because the relationship between sleep and stress is well-established in the clinical literature, making it a useful benchmark for verifying that the analysis produces plausible results.


Code
scores_sleep, loadings_sleep, vr_sleep = run_pca(df_sleep, "stress_level")
plot_variance(vr_sleep, "Sleep: PCA Explained Variance")








Figure 10: PCA explained variance for the sleep dataset.





Figure 10 shows the eigenvalue decay for the sleep dataset. A steep drop after PC1 indicates that one dominant pattern accounts for most of the structure; a gentler slope means the variance is spread more evenly across dimensions.


Code
plot_loadings(loadings_sleep, "Sleep: PC1 Loadings")








Figure 11: PC1 loadings for the sleep dataset.





Figure 11 identifies the health and lifestyle variables that define the primary direction of variation. Variables with large absolute loadings on PC1 are the main contributors to the dominant pattern in this dataset.


Code
plot_pc_scatter(scores_sleep, "Sleep: PC1 vs PC2")








Figure 12: PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the sleep dataset.





Figure 12 shows how the 374 observations distribute in the two-dimensional PCA space. The presence of visible bands or clusters would suggest that the dataset contains distinguishable subgroups (e.g., people with and without sleep disorders).

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
4.4 Dataset 4: Student Lifestyle
Target variable: stress_numeric | Source: Rogg (2023)


Code
corr_table(df_students, "stress_numeric")




Table 5: Pearson correlations of student lifestyle variables with stress_numeric.






Feature
Pearson r




Study_Hours_Per_Day
0.739


GPA
0.55


Extracurricular_Hours_Per_Day
-0.006


Social_Hours_Per_Day
-0.055


Physical_Activity_Hours_Per_Day
-0.205


Sleep_Hours_Per_Day
-0.299








Table 5 presents the associations for the student dataset. Variables such as study hours per day, sleep hours, and GPA are typically expected to relate to stress; the table shows whether these expectations are confirmed in this sample.


Code
plot_correlations(df_students, "stress_numeric", "Students: Correlation with Stress")








Figure 13: Pearson correlations between student lifestyle variables and stress_numeric.





Figure 13 shows which aspects of student life most strongly predict the numeric stress score. This comparison is interesting because student stress operates in a different context from clinical or workplace stress, yet some common factors (sleep, activity) may still emerge.


Code
scores_students, loadings_students, vr_students = run_pca(df_students, "stress_numeric")
plot_variance(vr_students, "Students: PCA Explained Variance")








Figure 14: PCA explained variance for the student lifestyle dataset.





The variance decomposition in Figure 14 indicates how many components are needed to capture the main structure of the student dataset. Because the dataset has only 6 predictor variables, the maximum number of components is 6.


Code
plot_loadings(loadings_students, "Students: PC1 Loadings")








Figure 15: PC1 loadings for the student lifestyle dataset.





Figure 15 shows which student lifestyle variables dominate PC1. If study hours and sleep hours load with opposite signs, it would suggest a trade-off pattern: students who study more tend to sleep less, and vice versa.


Code
plot_pc_scatter(scores_students, "Students: PC1 vs PC2")








Figure 16: PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the student lifestyle dataset.





Figure 16 maps the 2 000 student observations into the PC1–PC2 plane. A clear linear or curved structure would imply that student lifestyle profiles vary along a well-defined continuum.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
4.5 Dataset 5: Remote Work
Target variable: productivity_score | Source: A. (2024)


Code
corr_table(df_remote, "productivity_score")




Table 6: Pearson correlations of remote-work variables with productivity_score. Non-numeric columns and boolean indicators are excluded; ID column employee_id is also dropped.






Feature
Pearson r




experience_years
0.082


work_life_balance_rating
0.073


team_communication_effectiveness
0.025


hours_worked_per_week
-0.006


burnout_score
-0.015


sleep_hours_per_day
-0.018


age
-0.057


remote_setup_satisfaction
-0.125








Table 6 presents the correlations for the remote work dataset. With only 100 observations, individual correlations should be interpreted cautiously, but the overall pattern can still indicate which aspects of remote work conditions most closely align with productivity.


Code
plot_correlations(df_remote, "productivity_score", "Remote Work: Correlation with Productivity Score")








Figure 17: Pearson correlations between remote-work numeric variables and productivity_score.





Figure 17 shows whether variables such as work-life balance, sleep hours, and burnout score exhibit direct linear associations with the remote productivity score. A high absolute correlation for burnout would be consistent with the expectation that burnout and productivity are inversely related.


Code
scores_remote, loadings_remote, vr_remote = run_pca(df_remote, "productivity_score")
plot_variance(vr_remote, "Remote Work: PCA Explained Variance")








Figure 18: PCA explained variance for the remote work dataset.





Figure 18 shows how the 8 numeric predictor variables spread across principal components. Because the dataset is small (100 rows), PCA results should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.


Code
plot_loadings(loadings_remote, "Remote Work: PC1 Loadings")








Figure 19: PC1 loadings for the remote work dataset.





Figure 19 identifies which remote-work predictors define the dominant pattern. Variables loading strongly on PC1 are those that co-vary the most across the 100 observed employees.


Code
plot_pc_scatter(scores_remote, "Remote Work: PC1 vs PC2")








Figure 20: PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the remote work dataset.





Figure 20 shows the relatively small remote work sample in the two-dimensional PCA space. Outliers or tight clusters would warrant closer examination to understand whether they represent specific subgroups (e.g., by work mode or job role).

title: What Affects Well-Being?
text:
This project investigates which lifestyle, sleep, and work-related factors are statistically associated with human well-being, stress, and productivity. Five independently collected datasets are analysed using Pearson correlation and Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
Rather than merging all data into one table (which would introduce misleading cross-population comparisons), each dataset is examined separately and the results are compared at the end to surface recurring patterns.

title: What Affects Well-Being?
text:
This project investigates which lifestyle, sleep, and work-related factors are statistically associated with human well-being, stress, and productivity. Five independently collected datasets are analysed using Pearson correlation and Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
Rather than merging all data into one table (which would introduce misleading cross-population comparisons), each dataset is examined separately and the results are compared at the end to surface recurring patterns.

title: What Affects Well-Being?
text:
Datasets
Five cleaned datasets are used (see Analysis §Datasets for details):

Lifestyle & Wellness — screen time, sleep, exercise → wellness
Productivity & Habits — daily habits, sleep, stress → productivity
Sleep & Health — clinical sleep study → stress_level
Student Lifestyle — study load, GPA, activity → stress_numeric
Remote Work — work conditions, burnout → productivity_score

title: What Affects Well-Being?
text:
Methods
Two complementary methods are applied to every dataset:

Pearson correlation — identifies variables with the strongest direct linear relationship with the target variable.
PCA — reveals the dominant directions of variance and which original variables drive them.

Results do not imply causation; they describe statistical associations in the available samples.

title: What Affects Well-Being?
text:
Source Code & Data
All code, cleaned data, and rendered output are available on GitHub: github.com/caxarr/mood-analisis

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
Project Idea
I analysed which factors are related to well-being, stress, and productivity.
The main focus was on:

Sleep
Stress
Physical activity
Daily habits
Productivity and work-life balance

Students

I analysed the datasets separately because they describe different groups of people.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
Methods
I used two main methods:
Correlation analysis Shows which variables are directly related to the target.
PCA Shows the main structure of the dataset and reduces many variables into several principal components.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being

Remote Work Dataset
Target: productivity_score
This part focused on productivity in remote work conditions.

title: Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being
text:
Conclusion
The same types of variables appeared repeatedly:

Sleep (quality and duration)
Stress
Physical activity
Daily habits
Lifestyle or work-life balance

The analysis does not prove causality, but it shows clear statistical relationships in the available data.
